attributes.stem:
A 14-year-old boy presents with his parents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of facial acne. He reports both blackheads and red, painful spots on his face that sometimes contain pus. His parents has bought him the face washes from the chemist which he uses three times per day without any improvement in his symptoms. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple open and closed comedones on his forehead and cheeks and no similar lesions on his back or shoulders. There are no nodules or cysts, and no scarring is present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with moderate acne vulgaris (acne) characterised by both comedonal and inflammatory lesions. The presence of both types of lesions without nodules or scarring guides the treatment choice, however one should also consider any psychological impact that acne can have on the individual. Regardless of the treatment choice, patients should be reviewed in 6 weeks at least with ongoing review for treatment guidance.

Topical benzoyl peroxide plus adapalene would the recommended treatment for this case of a patient with moderate acne. Benzoyl peroxide has antimicrobial properties and helps reduce inflammation, while adapalene (a retinoid) helps prevent comedone formation and promotes existing comedone resolution. This combination addresses both the inflammatory and comedonal components of acne. Review would be completed at 6 weeks and then one would consider adding oral doxycycline if there is no improvement.

Oral doxycycline is secondly-line treatment when topical treatment was unsuccessful or there is scarring or severe acne. Female patients could also be offered the combined oral contraceptive pill which is shown to be beneficial in moderate acne management.

Oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe nodular acne or acne that has not been adequately managed with topical therapies or oral antibiotics. It is used under guidance from a Dermatologist. It has significant side effects, is teratogenic and requires careful monitoring. This patient's presentation does not warrant such aggressive treatment as yet given that he does not present with nodular acne or scarring.

Reassurance alone would be inappropriate given the moderate severity of acne and its potential psychological impact. Active treatment is warranted as acne can significantly affect quality of life and self-esteem in adolescents. Furthermore, early treatment can prevent scarring.

Topical salicylic acid face wash alone is unlikely to be sufficient for moderate acne. While it can help with mild comedonal acne, this patient's inflammatory lesions require more targeted therapy. It is likely also what he was using as over-the-counter washes which was insufficient management in his case.

Patients should also be counselled on the many myths of acne - acne is not due to being dirty, chocolate does not cause acne (though high sugar foods can worse acne for some patients), UV light is not an acne treatment and it is best to not squeeze acne lesions to prevent scarring.
learningPoints: Moderate acne should be managed with topical benzoyl peroxide plus a retinoid for 6 weeks initially.
Oral antibiotics or the combined oral contraceptive pill is second-line treatment when topical therapy was inadequate or for severe acne.
references: Cook D, Krassas G, Huang T. Acne: Best Practice Management. Australian Family Physician. 2010: 39 (9). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/41d41870-eba3-46c6-a7bf-26f2a3e2905f/Acne-best-practice-management.aspx

Reynolds R et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris.  Journal of American Academy of Dermatology.  2024: 90 (5).  Available at: https://www.jaad.org/article/S0190-9622(23)03389-3/fulltext
